THE BIG BLURB
“Paul Lotman has always been a guy who passed incredibly well, and that’s always important on any team. He’s an excellent, skilled volleyball player, a great teammate to have.” – U.S. Men’s Head Coach John Speraw in the Orange County Register

bio.education: Westside High School (Houston) '13; University of Houston '18
bio.fun_fact: Wrote a letter to the IOC asking them to add breaking to the future Olympics; later participated in the sport's inaugural competition at the Olympic Games Paris 2024
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • B-boy name is "Bboy Jeffro" 
  • Wrote a letter to the International Olympic Committee (IOC) asking them to add breaking to the future Olympics; later participated in the sport's inaugural competition at the Olympic Games
  • Creator of FitBreak, a fitness program that combines breaking and fitness exercises

  • Recipient of the 2025 Team USA Community Champions in partnership with Comcast award

Biographical Information

Jeffrey Louis was born and raised in Houston, Texas, the son of Haitian immigrants. His two older brothers, Pierry and Kenny, taught him breaking when he was in middle school, and he quickly earned the B-boy name “Bboy Jeffro” thanks to his “fro” at the time.

When the sport of breaking was first included in the Youth Olympic Games Buenos Aires 2018, Louis wrote an email to the International Olympic Committee (IOC) to encourage them to add breaking to future Olympics. Even though the IOC never wrote back to his letter, Louis did get his wish when, a year later, breaking was added to program of the Olympic Games Paris 2024.

In the lead-up to the Paris Games, Louis would win multiple silver medals for Team USA at the IWGA World Games Birmingham 2022 and Pan American Games Santiago 2023. Louis made his Olympic debut at the Paris Games, reaching the quarterfinals before being eliminated. 

Louis is also active with Houston reVision, a faith-based nonprofit that has been using weekly breaking sessions to build community and mentor kids for decades.  Louis has been active in the organization since he was a teenager, first improving his breaking moves there, and now gives back as an Olympian and through offering FitBreak (a fitness system that he created that combines breaking with fitness exercises) classes over Zoom to the kids currently involved with Houston reVision.

USA Track & Field

#26
Height: 5'7"
Position: Outfield - L/R
Hometown: Tustin, Calif.
School: Arizona
Graduation: 2007

A FEW ACCOMPLISHMENTS

Gold medalist at the Pan American Games (2007)
Four-time World Cup Champion (2006, 2007, 2009, 2010)
Two-time World Champion (2006, 2010)
Olympic Silver medalist (2008)

EXPERIENCE

2010: World Cup Champion
2009: Competed at Canada Cup, World Cup and Pan American Qualifier
- Made 29 starts in 30 games, most on the team, with a .420 average and a team-high 32 runs scored
- Was perfect in the outfield
2008: Silver medalist at the Olympic Games in Beijing, China
- Recorded the first ever inside the park home run in Olympic history, August 12, 2008, USA wins 11-0 over Venezuela
2007: Gold medalist at Pan American Games and World Cup Champion
- Hit .500 (8-for-16) with six runs scored and four stolen bases (World Cup)
- Led team over the summer with 27 hits; tied a team-high with two triples and a second- best 21 runs scored
- At Canada Cup, scored 12 runs with a .448 average (13-for-29); had a team-high two triples with eight RBI
2006: Gold medalist at ISF World Championship and World Cup Champion
- Hit .314 (11-for-35) with a team-tying high 14 runs scored; was a perfect 6-6 in stolen bases
- Hit .524 (11-for-21) and had an on-base-percentage of .545 with three stolen bases (World Cup)
2005: Silver medalist at Japan Cup and World Cup
- Hit .313 (5-16) with three runs (Japan)
- Hit .417 (5-12) with three runs (World Cup)
2004: Member of USA Schutt Elite Team at Canada Cup and Champions Cup
- Hit .404 (23-57) with a team-high 16 runs

AT ARIZONA
Two-time National Champion (2006 & 2007)
Four-time NFCA All-American (2004 – 2007)
Two-time Top Three Finalist for USA Softball Collegiate Player of the Year (2005 & 2007)
Two-time member of All-Women’s College World Series team (2006 & 2007)

GETTING TO KNOW LOWE

I love to lay on the beach and shop with my friends. I come from a large family. I have three sisters and a brother. I actually played against my little sister Paige, who plays for Oregon State, at the 2006 World Series. My favorite snack is Cheezits. I would love to have pet boxer puppies. I like watching the Yankees play. I hate watching the Red Sox win. If I could have dinner with anyone it would be my family. My favorite place to shop is Karmel and Alden Jean Co. My birthday is February 6, 1985. My good luck charm is my necklace. My biggest weaknesses are chocolate cake and Pepsi. I can’t stand scary movies. My favorite childhood memories are holidays with my family.

Most Notable

  • Bronze medalist at 2016 Olympic Games, playing a key reserve role coming off the bench to provide 41 points highlighted by a 13-point performance versus Serbia in semifinal and 12 points against Italy in pool play
  • Named MVP of 2015 FIVB World Grand Prix in First Tournament
  • Selected AVCA All-America First Team in 2014 as a senior at UCLA
  • Led all NCAA Division I players in kills per set (5.96) and points per set (6.60) in 2014

Major Competitions
2019 - Pan American Cup (Gold) ... FIVB World Cup (Silver) ... NORCECA Women's Continental Championship (Silver)
2018 - FIVB World Championship (5th Place)
2016 - NORCECA Olympic Qualification Tournament (Gold)...FIVB World Grand Prix (Silver)...Olympic Games (Bronze).
2015 - USA Volleyball Cup vs. China ... FIVB World Grand Prix (Gold)...FIVB World Cup (Bronze)...NORCECA Championship (Gold).

Did You Know

  • After 2016 Olympic Games, Lowe took a break from the U.S. Women's National Team to go back to school to pursue a master's of landscape architecture at University of Southern California
  • Never made the 'A' team until her junior year of high school
  • Selected Capital One Academic All-America Third Team by CoSIDA in 2014
  • Nominee for Lowe's Senior Class Award, the nation's premier award for NCAA Senior Student-Athletes
  • Played tennis competitively until middle of high school
  • Started playing indoor volleyball in 2003 with Coast Volleyball Club, but finished junior career playing with Wave Volleyball Club
  • Hobbies are hiking, tennis, DIY projects, ocean activities
  • Never lived outside of southern California and is about to live in a different country for eight months
  • Graduated from UCLA in 2015 with a bachelor's degree in geography/environmental studies

bio.education: Chatfield Senior HS (Littleton, Colo.) '89 Arapahoe Community College
bio.biography:
Sport: Alpine Skiing
Height: 5-10
Weight: 195
Classification: LW3, Standing
Date of Birth: Feb. 21, 1971
Birthplace: Denver, Colo.
Hometown: Littleton, Colo.
Current Residence: Littleton, Colo.
High School: Chatfield Senior HS (Littleton, Colo.) '89
College: Arapahoe Community College
Coach: Kevin Jardine
Military Service Branch: United States Marine Corps
Military Rank: Sergeant
Military Status: Retired

Career Highlights

  • 2014: Paralympic Winter Games competitor, super-G, giant slalom and slalom; World Cup Copper Mountain, 18th giant slalom run 1, 23rd giant slalom run 2; World Cup Panorama, 24th super-G; NorAm Winter Park, 4th slalom, 5th giant slalom run 1 and 2; U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championships, 1st downhill
  • 2013: IPC Alpine Skiing World Championships, 11th slalom; World Cup Thredbo, 3rd giant slalom, 4th slalom; U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championships, 1st super-G
  • 2012: U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championships, 1st downhill and super-G; Named to 2012-13 U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National B Team

Personal

Before getting involved in the Paralympic Movement, Jonathan Lujan served in the Marine Corps for more than eight and a half years, initially from 1993-97 and again after the September 11, 2001, attacks from 2001-2006. During a mission in Iraq in 2003, Lujan ruptured two discs in his back. The surgery to repair the discs in 2005 damaged Lujan’s spinal cord, causing temporary loss of feeling and movement below his waist. Though he is able to walk, he has permanent nerve damage and paralysis in his lower legs that restrict his movement and have left him with no feeling below his knees.

Lujan turned to ski racing in 2008 when fellow wounded veteran Nick Orchowiski recommended the National Disabled Veterans Winter Sports Clinic (NDVWSC) based in Snowmass Village, Colo. Lujan progressed quickly, joining the Aspen Valley Ski and Snowboard Club in the spring of 2011 as part of the U.S. Paralympic Developmental Program. By July 2012, he had made the U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National B Team roster.

Lujan served as the flag bearer for Team USA at the Opening Ceremony of the Sochi 2014 Paralympic Winter Games. He also serves as a role model for future Paralympians, children with disabilities, and injured servicemen and women as a Paralympic Ambassador. In addition, he remains a mentor for veterans currently training at the NDVWSC.

Lujan is a Littleton, Colo., native and a graduate of Chatfield Senior High School, where he was on the football, wrestling and track teams. Though he did not ski competitively until after his injury, Lujan has been an avid skier since childhood and has spent many of his winters on the mountain in Winter Park, Colo.

Connect

Facebook: Facebook.com/JonnyVolcano44
